app.title: Semantic Anchors
header.slogan: One word, and the AI gets the rest.
contracts.title: Semantic Contracts
contracts.explanation: Semantic Anchors reference public knowledge that LLMs already understand. But your team's conventions, templates, and definitions? Those need Semantic Contracts. A contract defines what a term means in your project — either by composing established anchors or by providing custom definitions that only exist within your team. Select the ones that fit and download them for your AGENTS.md or CLAUDE.md.
contracts.linkedinLink: Read the full story behind Semantic Contracts on LinkedIn →
main.heading: Explore Semantic Anchors
main.subheading: A curated catalog of well-defined terms, methodologies, and frameworks for effective LLM communication.
hero.title: No download. No install. Just type.
hero.intro: Semantic Anchors are words — established terms like Clean Architecture, Cockburn Use Cases or MECE — that activate rich, well-defined concepts inside any modern LLM.
hero.introEmphasis: You don't install them. You don't import them. You type them.
hero.example.1.without: Please refactor this code keeping the dependency rule in mind, with entities at the center, frameworks at the edge, and use cases in between, separating I/O concerns from domain logic, isolating the database via interfaces, ensuring the inner layers know nothing about the outer ones, and routing every framework call through an adapter so the core remains framework-agnostic and testable in isolation…
hero.example.1.expansion: The LLM expands the second prompt into the first — because Clean Architecture is in its training data.
hero.example.2.without: Make sure these categories don't overlap with each other and that together they cover the entire problem space — every option should fall into exactly one category, no possibility should be left out, the dimensions should be at the same level of abstraction, and the partitioning should hold even at the boundaries between categories…
hero.example.2.expansion: The LLM expands the second prompt into the first — because MECE is in its training data.
hero.example.3.without: Write the requirement as a structured scenario with primary actor, secondary actors, preconditions, trigger, the main success scenario as numbered atomic steps, extensions for alternative paths and error cases keyed by step number, postconditions on success and failure, and keep the whole thing at user-goal level rather than describing UI clicks or system internals…
hero.example.3.expansion: The LLM expands the second prompt into the first — because Cockburn Use Cases are in its training data.
hero.example.4.without: Model the core concepts as entities and value objects, group them into aggregates with one consistency boundary each, use the same vocabulary as the business stakeholders throughout the code, identify bounded contexts where the language and rules differ, draw context maps with explicit relationships, and place domain events on the seams between aggregates and contexts…
hero.example.4.expansion: The LLM expands the second prompt into the first — because Domain-Driven Design is in its training data.
hero.example.5.without: Write the acceptance criteria as scenarios with three labelled sections: the initial state of the system before the action, the single user action that triggers the behaviour, and the observable outcome that follows; one example per scenario, parameterise data via Examples tables when patterns repeat, group related scenarios under a Feature header with a narrative line stating role / goal / benefit…
hero.example.5.expansion: The LLM expands the second prompt into the first — because Gherkin is in its training data.
hero.example.6.without: Document the architecture in 12 sections covering introduction and goals, constraints, context, solution strategy, building blocks, runtime view, deployment view, crosscutting concepts, architecture decisions, quality requirements, risks and technical debt, and a glossary; keep each section to its purpose, link instead of repeat, and put diagrams next to the text that references them…
hero.example.6.expansion: The LLM expands the second prompt into the first — because arc42 is in its training data.
hero.step3Desc: it into your AI prompt — that's it.
footer.tagline: Semantic Anchors - Shared vocabulary for LLM communication

onboarding.slogan2: One word, and the AI gets the rest.
onboarding.text0: You don't download or install Semantic Anchors. They live inside any modern AI model — you just need the right word.
onboarding.text1: Imagine saying just one word – and your counterpart instantly understands an entire concept.
onboarding.text2: A Semantic Anchor is an established term that activates an entire body of knowledge. Like an anchor holding a ship in place – a Semantic Anchor pins your conversation to a precise concept.
onboarding.text3: This works because AI models were trained on millions of texts. Terms like MECE, Clean Architecture, or the Feynman Technique instantly trigger deep contextual knowledge.
onboarding.text4: Instead of writing long prompts, just use the right anchor – and the AI delivers.
